results.title: CRAF'd - The Complex Risk Analytics Fund
results.organization: Germany, United States, United Kingdom, Luxembourg, Netherlands, European Commission, Canada
theme: Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations
results.proposal_summary: CRAF'd (Complex Risk Analytics Fund) is a multi-partner initiative funded by Germany, the US, the UK, Luxembourg, the Netherlands, the European Commission, and Canada, focused on financing crisis data commons, analytics, and AI to support anticipation, prevention, and response to complex risks. The website is an institutional homepage describing the fund's mission, ecosystem, and past projects. No active Call for Proposals with defined eligibility, funding amounts, application process, or submission deadline is currently published, making this page non-actionable as a grant opportunity.
results.ilo_match_evidence: CRAF'd focuses on crisis data, analytics, and AI for humanitarian action rather than ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work, though some thematic overlap exists around crisis-affected populations.

results.classification_summary: The EU Delegation to Afghanistan has launched a Call for Expression of Interest focused on addressing drivers of migration and forced displacement through economic stabilisation and community resilience in Afghanistan, with a total budget of EUR 40 million and a submission deadline of 5 June 2026. However, the eligible applicant category is described only as 'organisations meeting the eligibility criteria' without explicitly naming UN agencies, international organisations, or IGOs; the detailed guidelines PDF could not be retrieved to confirm ILO-type entity eligibility, so ILO eligibility cannot be established as true and defaults to false under the exhaustive-list rule.

results.title: Call for Expression of Interest: Addressing the drivers of migration and forced displacement by supporting economic stabilization and strengthening community resilience (AFG/2026/CfEoI)
results.deadline_text: Friday, June 5, 2026, at 17:00 (Brussels date and time)
theme: Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations
organization_type: Organisations meeting the eligibility criteria (details in Guidelines)

application_process.description: Registration deadline for online information session (email to delegation-afghanistan-CfEoI-1-2026@eeas.europa.eu by 17:00 CET)
Online information session at 10:00 Brussels time
Submission deadline for concept note at 17:00 Brussels time

eligible_activities: Addressing drivers of migration and forced displacement
Supporting economic stabilisation in Afghanistan
Strengthening community resilience

results.proposal_summary: The EU Delegation to Afghanistan has issued a Call for Expression of Interest (Reference: AFG/2026/Call for expression of interest) with a total budget of EUR 40 million to address the drivers of migration and forced displacement in Afghanistan by supporting economic stabilisation and strengthening community resilience. The call is part of the EU's ongoing support for basic needs and livelihoods in Afghanistan. Organisations meeting the eligibility criteria (as specified in the downloadable Guidelines) are invited to submit a concept note by 5 June 2026 at 17:00 Brussels time. An online information session will be held on 12 May 2026; interested organisations must register by 11 May 2026. The call targets activities in Afghanistan, and eligible applicant types are detailed in the Guidelines PDF. No minimum grant size per award or co-financing requirement is stated on the main page.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call addresses labour migration drivers, economic stabilisation, and community resilience in a crisis context, which partially aligns with ILO mandates on labour migration, employment policy, and decent work in crisis situations, though the primary focus is on humanitarian/displacement rather than core labour standards.

results.proposal_summary: The European Union, through the EU-Philippine Green Economy Partnership (EUR 60 million total), is funding the Green Up to Scale Up: MSME Transformation Grant, managed by Expertise France in partnership with the Philippine Department of Trade and Industry. The grant offers EUR 1.8 million (PHP 125 million) in non-reimbursable financial assistance and technical support to networks of actors supporting the circular/green transformation of Filipino MSMEs in the agriculture and fisheries, tourism and hospitality, food, and textile sectors. Eligible applicants are private-sector entities and business support networks operating in the Philippines. The application deadline was extended to 30 April 2026 (now passed). No implementation period or co-financing requirements are specified in the available text.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses on green/circular economy transformation of MSMEs in the Philippines, which has limited direct alignment with ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, social protection, and social dialogue, though sustainable enterprise development is tangentially related.

results.title: Building Stronger Frontline Media and Informed Communities – FAIR Media Ukraine Grants Competition
results.deadline_text: Deadline: 11 May, 2026, 18:00 (Kyiv time)
theme: Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations

application_process.description: Submission of project proposals by 18:00 Kyiv time

eligible_activities: Regular production and distribution of local news or public-interest content relevant to frontline or war-affected communities
Revenue diversification and audience engagement strategies
Sales or fundraising capacity building
Operational efficiency improvements
Strengthening staff qualifications and retention
Content production and distribution costs
Core staff and contributor costs
Tools, equipment, and/or expertise that improve media outlets' sustainability
funding_restrictions: Applicants must not have previously received a grant from the EU-funded FAIR Media Ukraine project
Applicants must be registered in Ukraine and serve audiences in the specified frontline regions
Must maintain operational presence in the regions served
results.proposal_summary: The European Union, through Internews International's FAIR Media Ukraine project, invites Ukrainian media outlets from eleven frontline regions (Donetsk, Luhansk, Kharkiv, Sumy, Chernihiv, Zaporizhzhia, Kherson, Mykolaiv, Odesa, Dnipropetrovsk, and Kyiv) to apply for grants averaging EUR 15,000 each for projects lasting up to eight months. The competition aims to support regional and local media operating under wartime conditions to continue delivering trusted, relevant information and strengthen their medium-term resilience. Eligible applicants must be media outlets registered in Ukraine that serve frontline audiences, demonstrate editorial independence, maintain an operational presence in their covered regions, and must not have previously received a FAIR Media Ukraine grant. The deadline for submission is 11 May 2026 at 18:00 Kyiv time. No co-financing is required. The ILO is not eligible as applicants are restricted to Ukrainian-registered media outlets, and the grant size is below the USD 50,000 threshold.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This grant focuses on media sustainability and wartime information provision in Ukraine's frontline regions, which does not align with the ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The CfP explicitly targets 'civil society organisations' (CSOs). The ILO, as an international/intergovernmental organization and UN agency, does not fall within this eligible applicant category.

results.proposal_summary: The EU Delegation to Malaysia has launched a Call for Proposals in 2026 targeting eligible civil society organisations to submit innovative projects aimed at enhancing CSOs' contribution to governance and development processes. The call is managed by the EU Delegation to Malaysia, with contact routed through the Thailand Regional Call for Proposals email. results.classification_summary: This is an EU-funded Call for Proposals under the Thematic Programme on Human Rights and Democracy, targeting CSOs operating in Tajikistan to promote human rights, fundamental freedoms, and rule of law. The call specifies support for CSO actions, which excludes ILO as an international/UN organization. results.proposal_summary: The Delegation of the European Union to Uganda has launched a €5.1 million in-country Call for Proposals under the CSO Thematic Programme 2021-2027 (EuropeAid/186375/DD/ACT/Multi). The call targets Civil Society Organisations and aims to: (1) promote sustainable, inclusive and rights-based management of the environment and natural resources, focusing on forests, land use and related value chains; and (2) contribute to a more inclusive and equitable local economy in areas where the EU and Team Europe are engaged in Global Gateway investments in renewable energy, including through enhanced access to clean energy, decent work and diversified livelihoods in Uganda. The call is restricted to CSOs; international organizations such as the ILO are not eligible applicants. The deadline for submission of proposals is 4 June 2026, with an information session scheduled for 6 May 2026.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call has partial relevance to ILO mandate through its 'decent work and diversified livelihoods' component, but is primarily focused on environmental/natural resource management and clean energy, and is restricted to CSOs, excluding the ILO.

results.title: Adaptation Benefit Mechanism (ABM) - African Development Bank
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies
eligible_activities: Solar powered irrigation pumps
Drip irrigation technology

Climate resilient agriculture
Weather information systems
Job creation for vulnerable groups affected by climate hazards

Clean energy technologies
results.proposal_summary: The Adaptation Benefit Mechanism (ABM) is an initiative of the African Development Bank (AfDB) designed to mobilize public and private sector finance for climate change adaptation in Africa through a results-based finance model. The mechanism certifies social, economic, and environmental adaptation benefits and promotes these certificates to potential investors or lenders. Piloted from 2019 to 2023 across approximately 10-12 demonstration projects in Africa (now concluded), ABM covers a broad scope including resilient agriculture, coastal protection, clean energy, and weather information systems. This page is an informational overview of the mechanism and does not constitute an open Call for Proposals with a defined grant amount, eligible applicant categories, or submission deadline. The pilot phase has concluded and a permanent secretariat is being established by 2027.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The ABM focuses on climate change adaptation finance in Africa with some references to job creation for vulnerable groups and women's economic empowerment, which tangentially aligns with ILO's just transitions and decent work mandates, but the mechanism is primarily an environmental finance tool with no direct focus on labour standards, employment policy, or social protection.

On the basis of available evidence, the ILO (as a UN/international organisation) does not fall within the eligible applicant category, which is restricted to sovereign African states/governments. The funding instrument includes a grant component. ILO Eligibility fails because the programme targets ADF-eligible member countries, not international organisations.

results.proposal_summary: The ADF Climate Action Window is a dedicated financing facility established by the African Development Fund (ADF) under its 16th replenishment cycle to address the approximately $60 billion annual climate financing gap faced by low-income African countries. The Window allocates resources across three sub-windows: 75% for adaptation, 15% for mitigation, and 10% for technical assistance, covering six thematic sectors including agriculture and food security, water security, climate information and early warning, green transport and infrastructure, green energy and energy efficiency, and green finance. Eligible recipients are ADF-member low-income African countries (sub-Saharan Africa and other ADF-eligible states). The facility offers a large grant component to minimize debt distress risks. No specific submission deadline or maximum grant size is stated in the available text. As the programme targets sovereign governments rather than international organisations, the ILO as a UN agency would not qualify as an eligible applicant.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the ADF Climate Action Window touches on just transition themes (green energy, green infrastructure, adaptation), it is primarily a sovereign climate finance facility for African governments and does not align with the ILO's core mandate areas of labour standards, employment, social protection, or social dialogue.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Adaptation Acceleration Program (AAAP) is a joint initiative of the African Development Bank (AfDB) and the Global Center on Adaptation (GCA), aiming to mobilize $25 billion over five years to accelerate climate adaptation across Africa through four pillars: climate-smart digital agriculture (targeting 30 million farmers in 26 countries), infrastructure resilience, youth empowerment for green jobs (1 million youth), and innovative adaptation finance. The program operates through an AAAP Upstream Financing Facility at GCA and the ADF Climate Action Window providing up to $13 billion for low-income African countries. This page is an informational program description rather than a Call for Proposals; no submission deadline, eligible applicant categories, or specific grant size for external applicants are defined. The ILO contributed the 'Jobs' chapter to the associated State and Trends in Adaptation 2021 report, indicating potential thematic alignment.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The AAAP focuses primarily on climate adaptation and resilience infrastructure finance in Africa; while one pillar addresses youth employment and green jobs (an ILO mandate area), the program is not a labour/decent work-focused CfP and does not constitute an open grant call for which ILO could apply.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Climate Change Fund (ACCF) is a multi-donor trust fund administered by the African Development Bank, established in 2014 with contributions from Germany, Belgium/Flanders, Italy, Canada, Austria, Ireland, the United States, the Province of Quebec, and the Global Center on Adaptation. The ACCF provides small grants to African governments, NGOs, and regional institutions to support Africa's transition towards climate-resilient, low-carbon development, with a focus on accessing climate finance, implementing NDCs, developing Long-Term Strategies, and undertaking small-scale adaptation and mitigation actions while promoting gender equality. The fund covers all African Development Bank regional member countries. No specific open call for proposals deadline, minimum/maximum grant amounts, or application process are stated in the publicly available page content. ILO as an international organization is not among the eligible applicant categories explicitly listed.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the ACCF focuses on climate change adaptation and mitigation in Africa (related to ILO's just transition mandate), its core focus is environmental/climate finance rather than labour standards, decent work, or social protection, and ILO is ineligible as an applicant.

results.title: Africa Disaster Risk Financing Programme (ADRiFi) Multi-Donor Trust Fund
theme: Just Transitions towards Environmentally Sustainable Economies and Societies
Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Disaster Risk Financing Programme (ADRiFi), initiated by the African Development Bank (AfDB) in 2018, aims to enhance the resilience of African countries to climate shocks by improving disaster risk management and financing. The programme's Multi-Donor Trust Fund (MDTF), established in 2021 and funded by the United Kingdom, Switzerland, Canada, Norway, the Netherlands, and the United States (~USD 96.7 million total), provides premium subsidies and capacity development support to AfDB Regional Member Countries (sovereign governments) across Africa. It is implemented in partnership with the African Risk Capacity (ARC) Group. All AfDB Regional Member Countries are eligible upon formal request to the Bank and meeting requirements for participation in the ARC regional risk pool. This is not an open Call for Proposals for external organizations; there is no defined application deadline or per-organization grant size. The ILO is not an eligible applicant under this programme framework.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While ADRiFi touches on livelihoods and food security for vulnerable rural workers (including women in agriculture), its core focus is on climate disaster risk financing and insurance for sovereign governments, which falls outside the ILO's primary mandate of labour standards, employment, and social protection.

theme: Decent Work in Crisis and Post-Crisis Situations

results.proposal_summary: The African Development Bank launched the USD 1.5 billion African Emergency Food Production Facility in May 2022 in response to food supply disruptions caused by the Russia-Ukraine war. The initiative aims to provide certified seeds and fertilizers to 20 million smallholder farmers across Africa, targeting a production increase of 38 million tonnes of food within two years. The facility is a sovereign lending and investment instrument directed at African member states and farmers; it is not an open call for proposals from NGOs, international organizations, or other external entities. No eligibility criteria for external applicants, no submission deadline, and no grant window for third parties are defined.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The facility addresses food security and agricultural resilience in Africa, which is tangentially related to ILO themes of decent work in crisis situations and rural employment, but its core focus is agricultural production finance rather than labour standards, social protection, or employment policy.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Fertilizer Financing Mechanism (AFFM) is a special fund administered by the African Development Bank Group, established by the African Union in 2006 to address low fertilizer use and stagnant agricultural productivity in Africa. The mechanism provides innovative financing solutions—primarily trade credit guarantees and grants—to accelerate fertilizer access and use in AfDB Regional Member Countries (RMCs) across the continent. The AFFM operates through a 2022–2028 strategic plan with three pillars: (i) strengthening the fertilizer sector through access to finance; (ii) supporting policy reforms; and (iii) facilitating smallholder farmers' access to inputs and technical assistance. Active projects have been implemented in Tanzania, Kenya, Uganda, Mozambique, Zimbabwe, Côte d'Ivoire, Ghana, and Nigeria. The 2024 work program budget totals USD 18.58 million, including USD 1.20 million for new activities. This is not an open, competitive Call for Proposals; it is an institutional program page describing an ongoing AfDB financing mechanism. No submission deadline or open application process for external organizations (such as the ILO) exists. The mechanism is ineligible for ILO participation as it targets African national implementing partners, financial institutions, and agro-dealers in RMCs.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The AFFM focuses on agricultural fertilizer financing and food security in Africa, which has minimal direct alignment with ILO's core mandate on labour standards, employment, social protection, and decent work, though there is a very marginal connection through rural employment and smallholder farmer livelihoods.

results.proposal_summary: The African Green Banks Initiative (AGBI) is a pan-African initiative led by the African Development Bank, launched at COP27, in partnership with the Climate Investment Funds, Canada Climate Action, the Green Bank Network, Amundi, and the Multilateral Cooperation Center for Development Finance (MCDF). Its objective is to create an ecosystem of Green Banks across Africa by mobilising additional climate finance for green and sustainable growth aligned with countries' Nationally Determined Contributions. The initiative targets a $1.5 billion ecosystem of green investment facilities by 2030 through a blended finance approach comprising Technical Assistance and a Green Finance Facility. It is active across all African countries. This page is an informational overview — it does not constitute a Call for Proposals and provides no grant application process, eligible applicant list, or submission deadline.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The AGBI focuses on climate finance mobilisation and green banking in Africa, which has only tangential relevance to ILO's mandate on decent work and just transitions, but does not directly address labour standards, employment, or social protection.

results.proposal_summary: The Africa Trade Fund (AfTra) is a multi-million dollar trade-related technical assistance facility established by the African Development Bank (AfDB) in March 2012, with seed financing from the Government of Canada. Its objective is to accelerate the integration of African countries and regional economic communities (RECs) into regional and global trading systems, complementing the Bank's operations in agriculture, infrastructure, governance, private sector development, and science and technology. The fund covers all African Development Bank member countries across the continent. No active Call for Proposals, submission deadline, per-grant funding amount, or applicant eligibility criteria are currently specified on the fund's webpage, making this an informational/program overview page rather than an open funding opportunity as of April 2026.

results.proposal_summary: The African Beneficial Ownership Transparency Network (AfBOTN) is an initiative launched in 2023 by the African Development Bank in partnership with the UK Foreign, Commonwealth and Development Office (FCDO) and other partners. Its purpose is to bring together international partners and African governments to support regional priorities in driving reforms around beneficial ownership transparency across Africa, combating illicit financial flows, money laundering, and corruption. The network focuses on peer learning, information sharing, and practical guidance for implementing beneficial ownership registers across African countries. This page is an informational description of the network initiative and does not constitute a Call for Proposals; no grant funding, application deadline, eligible applicant categories, or grant amounts are specified.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the initiative relates to governance and anti-corruption in Africa, it does not directly address ILO core mandate areas such as labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The African Carbon Support Program is an informational program page from the African Development Bank (AfDB), describing a technical assistance initiative launched on 4 November 2010 and implemented by AfDB's Energy, Environment and Climate Change Department. The program, funded by a one-time US$1 million grant from the Fund for African Private Sector Assistance (FAPA, established by AfDB and the Japanese government), aims to assist AfDB regional member countries in accessing carbon finance under the Clean Development Mechanism (CDM). Activities include screening investment proposals for carbon finance potential, building capacity of Designated National Authorities, developing project documentation (PINs and PDDs), supporting regional grid emission factor development, and helping project owners commercialize carbon credits. This is not an open Call for Proposals; there is no submission deadline, no defined eligible applicant categories, and no open grant competition. The ILO and other external organizations cannot apply.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The program focuses on carbon finance and climate change mitigation under the CDM in Africa, which has only marginal connection to ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, and decent work, though just transition themes are tangentially relevant.

results.proposal_summary: UK Aid Match is a grant programme administered by the UK Foreign, Commonwealth & Development Office (FCDO), implemented through fund manager MannionDaniels, providing match-funding grants to UK-based civil society organisations, NGOs, and humanitarian relief organisations for poverty reduction projects in developing countries across Africa, Asia, the Middle East, and beyond. For every £1 raised by a UK charity from the British public, the UK government contributes £1 of UK aid. Grants range from over £100,000 to more than £1,000,000 GBP. Eligible sectors include health, education, WASH, livelihoods, climate change, disabilities, and humanitarian response. Applicants must be UK-based non-profit or civil society organisations; international organizations such as the ILO are not eligible. No current round deadline is specified on the page; applications are made via www.ukaidmatch.org. The programme also supports Disasters Emergency Committee appeals at times of humanitarian crisis.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the programme funds livelihoods, social protection, and development projects broadly aligned with decent work themes, it is specifically directed at UK-based civil society charities for poverty reduction and humanitarian response, with limited focus on ILO core mandate areas such as labour standards, employment policy, social dialogue, or just transitions.

results.proposal_summary: The Brazil-UK PACT programme, funded by the UK Government through FCDO and DESNZ under the UK's International Climate Finance, launched a Call for Proposals on 31 October 2025 to fund one initiative focused on climate policy and governance in Brazil. The call sought projects that strengthen federative climate governance and support the implementation of Brazil's Climate Plan (NDC), including capacity building of public managers, fostering sub-national collaboration, and aligning national climate goals with sub-national plans. Eligible applicants included local and international NGOs, think-tanks, consultancies, academic institutions, professional associations, private sector entities, and UN agencies, with the lead organisation required to hold a valid Brazilian CNPJ registration. No funding amount was specified. The submission deadline was 15 December 2025, and the call is now closed.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses primarily on climate governance and NDC implementation in Brazil, with capacity building for public managers as a secondary element; while it touches on just transition themes adjacent to ILO's mandate, it does not directly address labour standards, employment, social protection, or decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI) – Haiti (2025), funded by Global Affairs Canada, supports small-scale, high-impact development projects in Haiti that align with Canada's international development thematic priorities. results.proposal_summary: The RISE (Resilient, Inclusive and Sustainable Environments) Grants Challenge 2025, managed by IUCN with funding from Norway/Norad, sought to fund projects addressing gender-based violence (GBV) risk mitigation embedded in environmental and climate programmes in 23 eligible countries across Africa, Asia, and Latin America. The call was open to a wide range of legally registered organisations including NGOs, CSOs, IGOs, IPOs, academic institutions, and private-sector entities, provided they had a local presence in the country of implementation. Projects were required to focus on GBV risk mitigation in or around Indigenous-managed lands, protected areas, or OECMs, and to partner with environmental, GBV-expert, and community organisations. The grant amount was not specified. The proposal submission deadline was 15 September 2025, and the call is now closed.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses narrowly on gender-based violence and environment/conservation linkages, which is peripheral to the ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, and social protection, though it touches on gender equality.

results.exclusion_reason: Deadline is in the future (opens 11 Aug 2025, deadline 15 Sep 2025) — the application window has not yet opened as of today (2026-05-11). Wait: the deadline was 15 Sep 2025, which is in the past relative to today's date of 2026-05-11. The call is closed.
results.classification_summary: The RISE grants challenge 2025 application deadline was 15 September 2025, which has already passed as of the current date (2026-05-11), rendering the call closed and ineligible. Additionally, while IGOs are listed as eligible applicants in the call for proposals document, the call is no longer open for submission. The call is therefore ineligible on the basis of a past deadline.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The opportunity is described as a grants challenge with non-reimbursable awards of USD 100,000–300,000; grant agreements are signed with awardees.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: Grant amounts range from USD 100,000 to USD 300,000, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The application submission deadline was 15 September 2025 (or 08 September 2025 per the detailed CfP document), which has passed as of today (2026-05-11).

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The application window opened 11 August 2025 and closed 15 September 2025. As of 2026-05-11, the call is closed and grant agreements were to be signed by February 2026.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: Section 6.2 mentions 'Cost-share' as a topic but does not mandate co-financing as a condition for eligibility based on available text.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No pre-existing accreditation with a body such as GEF or GCF is required; applicants must be legally registered organizations meeting programmatic requirements.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: Partnerships are required between environmental organisations, GBV experts, IPOs, etc., but no rule specifying that consortium leadership must be held by a non-UN entity is stated.

results.proposal_summary: The RISE (Resilient, Inclusive and Sustainable Environments) grants challenge 2025 Call for Proposals, managed by IUCN with funding from Norway/Norad, invites applications from legally registered organisations — including IGOs, NGOs, CBOs, CSOs, IPOs, universities, research institutes, and private sector entities — to implement projects addressing gender-based violence and environment linkages in up to 23 targeted countries. Grants of USD 100,000–300,000 each (up to USD 600,000 total, for up to 3 awards) support projects of 12–18 months that embed GBV risk mitigation in environmental or climate programmes, with a specific focus on the fisheries sector. Applicants must have local presence or a registered local partner in the country of implementation, and at least one lead or partner organisation must be an IUCN member, past RISE grantee, or past/present EbA Fund grantee. The application deadline was 8 September 2025 via the SMApply online portal; this call is now closed.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While this call touches on gender equality (a cross-cutting ILO concern) and the fisheries sector, its primary focus is on gender-based violence and environment/biodiversity linkages rather than core ILO mandate areas such as labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or social dialogue.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The call is open only to invited non-governmental organisations as lead applicants. While consortia may include multilateral/intergovernmental organisations, the ILO cannot serve as lead applicant, and the call is explicitly restricted to invited NGOs as leads. Additionally, this is a Stage 2 by-invitation-only call, meaning the first required step (receiving an invitation in November 2025) has already passed.
results.classification_summary: This is a Stage 2 by-invitation-only call from Norad (Norway) for projects to reduce plastic pollution and marine litter in developing countries (Indonesia, Vietnam, Kenya, Ghana, Tanzania, plus global/regional). Only invited NGOs may lead applications; ILO as an international organization is ineligible as lead applicant. The deadline is listed as 'Ongoing' but is a restricted/invited process where invitations were issued in November 2025. ILO could only participate as a consortium member, not as lead. The call is not aligned with ILO's core mandate of labour standards and decent work.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call explicitly states: 'This call is only open to invited non-governmental organisations as lead applicants.' ILO (an international/intergovernmental organization) is listed only as an eligible consortium participant, not as an eligible lead applicant. The eligible lead applicant category is strictly NGOs.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: Norad calls for proposals are non-reimbursable development grants. The page references the Grants Portal and grant management, indicating this is a grant instrument.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No specific grant size per project is mentioned in the page text. The overall programme has a 1 billion NOK ambition for 2025-2028, but individual grant amounts are not specified.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline is listed as 'Ongoing' with no specific date. However, this is a by-invitation-only Stage 2 process where invitations were issued in November 2025 (past). The actual submission deadline is not publicly stated.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a call for proposals for development grants, not a procurement tender or consultancy RFP.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The page does not explicitly mention co-financing requirements. The full call text is only available to invited applicants.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is mentioned as a requirement.

results.proposal_summary: Norad (Norway) is running Stage 2 of a by-invitation-only call for proposals under the Norwegian Development Programme to Reduce Plastic Pollution and Marine Litter (2025–2028), with an overall programme ambition of NOK 1 billion. The programme aims to reduce plastic pollution and marine litter in developing countries, with priority focus on Indonesia, Vietnam, Kenya, Ghana, and Tanzania, as well as global and regional projects. Only invited non-governmental organisations may serve as lead applicants; consortia are encouraged and may include for-profit entities, foreign governmental institutions, multilateral and intergovernmental organisations as participants. Individual grant amounts are not publicly specified. The deadline is listed as 'Ongoing' for invited applicants, with invitations having been issued in November 2025. ILO is ineligible as a lead applicant but could participate as a consortium member.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call focuses on plastic pollution and marine litter reduction in developing countries, with only a minor tangential link to ILO's mandate through the 'safe job creation in the circular economy' outcome; it does not primarily address labour standards, decent work, employment policy, or social protection.

results.exclusion_reason: Deadline has passed: the submission deadline of 6/3/2026 (interpreted as March 6, 2026 in DD/MM/YYYY Norwegian date format, or confirmed as past given the publication date context) — however re-evaluating: the publication date '4/8/2026' in Norwegian format would be April 8, 2026, which aligns with today. If the deadline '6/3/2026' is in Norwegian DD/MM/YYYY format, it would be June 3, 2026 (future). But 6 March 2026 would be before publication date of 8 April 2026, making no sense. Therefore deadline is June 3, 2026 (future). However, ILO eligibility is the exclusion reason: the full CfP text in the Grants Portal is inaccessible, but the visible text targets 'organisations working in Afghanistan' and the regulatory framework (Regionbevilgningene) typically lists eligible applicants as NGOs and civil society organisations, not international/intergovernmental organisations. Without explicit inclusion of ILO/international organizations in the accessible text, ILO eligibility is uncertain. Setting eligible=false due to ILO eligibility uncertainty combined with the strict exhaustive list rule.
results.classification_summary: This Norad call supports community-led approaches to address basic human needs in Afghanistan. Deadline is June 3, 2026 (future). The full eligibility criteria are only available in the Grants Portal (inaccessible). The visible text does not explicitly list eligible applicant types, making ILO eligibility unknown. The grant scheme (Regionbevilgningene/budget item 159.72) typically targets NGOs and civil society organisations. Since the text does not explicitly include international/UN organisations as eligible applicants in the accessible portion, ILO eligibility is classified as unknown/false under the strict exhaustive list rule.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The full call text is only in the Grants Portal (inaccessible). The visible Norad page text does not list eligible applicant types, but references the 'Regionbevilgningene' grant scheme rules. The budget template and supporting scheme rules (for other schemes) list eligible recipients as NGOs and civil society organisations. No explicit inclusion of international organisations/UN agencies is visible. Under the strict exhaustive list rule, ILO cannot be assumed eligible without explicit inclusion.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: This is a Norad grant scheme (tilskuddsordning) – non-reimbursable development aid grant. The budget template shows 'Grant funding Norad' and 'TOTAL NORAD GRANT AMOUNT', confirming this is a non-repayable grant.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No minimum or maximum grant amount is stated in the visible portion of the call for proposals. The full details are in the inaccessible Grants Portal.

Deadline is future.evidence: Deadline stated as '6/3/2026'. The publication date '4/8/2026' in the same format corresponds to April 8, 2026 (MM/DD/YYYY US format) or August 4, 2026 (DD/MM/YYYY). Given the page was published before today (April 22, 2026), the US format interpretation (April 8, 2026) is consistent. Thus the deadline '6/3/2026' = June 3, 2026, which is in the future relative to today (April 22, 2026).

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a call for proposals for development grants, not a procurement/tender for goods or services.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No mandatory co-financing requirement is mentioned in the visible text. The budget template has a field for 'Funding provided by grant recipient' but this appears optional.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules are mentioned in the accessible text.

results.proposal_summary: Norway (Norad), through the Regionbevilgningene grant scheme (budget item 159.72), issues a call for proposals to support rights-based, conflict-sensitive and inclusive economic and social development in Afghanistan, with a focus on community-led approaches to address basic human needs and improve well-being and resilience. The call is not open to international organisations such as the ILO, as the eligible applicant types (drawn from the Regionbevilgningene framework) are typically limited to NGOs and civil society organisations.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call addresses basic human needs and resilience in a crisis context (Afghanistan), which has some overlap with ILO's decent work in crisis mandate, the focus is primarily on community-led social/humanitarian development rather than core ILO themes of labour standards, employment, social dialogue, or social protection systems.

results.classification_summary: This is a Norwegian government (Norad/MFA) grant call for private sector development in Ukraine, with a total envelope of 100 million NOK for 2026-2027. The deadline is 17 June 2026 (future). Eligible applicants include multilateral organisations, Norwegian and foreign non-profit organisations, Norwegian and foreign commercial organisations, and state organisations. The ILO, as a multilateral organisation, fits within the listed eligible applicant categories. The funding is non-reimbursable grant support. The grant size exceeds USD 50,000. All hard exclusion criteria pass. The call focuses on investment-ready projects, private investment mobilisation, job creation, sustainable energy, food security, agriculture, and reconstruction of critical infrastructure in Ukraine.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The grant scheme regulations (Document 1) explicitly list 'Multilaterale organisasjoner' (Multilateral organisations) as potential grant recipients. The ILO, as a UN multilateral organisation, fits within this category.

Tilskuddsmottaker skal ikke oppnå profitt av tilskuddet' - grants cover actual costs with no profit, confirming non-reimbursable grant nature.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: The total envelope is 100 million NOK for 2026 and 2027, which is approximately USD 9-10 million at current exchange rates, well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline is stated as 6/17/2026 (June 17, 2026), which is after today's date of April 22, 2026.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body (e.g., GEF, GCF) is required. Applicants must demonstrate technical and administrative competence and capacity, and meet organisational criteria, but no external accreditation is mandated.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The FAQ states consortia apply through one lead organisation, but there is no restriction preventing a UN entity from being the lead. No rule requiring non-UN consortium leadership is mentioned.

results.proposal_summary: Norway (via Norad and the Ministry of Foreign Affairs) is allocating 100 million NOK for private sector development in Ukraine in 2026 and 2027, under the Nansen Support Programme for Ukraine (budget item 159.73). The call aims to promote investment-ready projects, facilitate increased private investment in Ukraine, mobilise private capital, create new jobs, and improve framework conditions for the private sector. Priority sectors include sustainable energy production, food security and agriculture, and reconstruction of critical infrastructure. The call is open to all applicants from any country (Ukrainian, international, and Norwegian), including multilateral organisations, non-profit organisations, commercial organisations, and state organisations that are legal entities with demonstrated technical and administrative competence. Applications must be submitted via the Grants Portal by 17 June 2026. Co-financing is not mandatory but can be included. Indirect costs are capped at 7% of direct operational costs. There is no explicitly stated minimum grant size; the total pool is 100 million NOK across 2026-2027.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While the call explicitly targets private sector development and job creation in Ukraine—which touches on ILO mandate areas of employment and enterprise development—it is primarily focused on investment mobilisation and commercial enterprise rather than labour standards, social protection, social dialogue, or core decent work elements that are central to the ILO's mandate.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The call requires applicants to be legal entities with a Canada Revenue Agency (CRA) number for Canadian organizations, implying the eligible applicant pool is limited to legal entities (NGOs, civil society, academic institutions, etc.) that can comply with Canadian contribution agreement terms. The ILO as a UN/international organization is not listed among eligible entity types and the requirement for a CRA number or equivalent legal entity status effectively excludes international organizations. Additionally, this is a rolling/unsolicited mechanism with no specific deadline, but the funding quantum and eligible applicant criteria do not accommodate UN agencies.
results.classification_summary: This is Global Affairs Canada's (GAC) unsolicited concept note funding mechanism for international assistance projects aligned with Canada's Feminist International Assistance Policy. It is a two-stage grant process (concept note → full proposal) open to legal entities. The ILO, as a UN/international organization, is not within the eligible applicant categories implied by the requirement for legal entity status with a CRA number. No specific grant size minimum/maximum is stated. There is no fixed submission deadline. The ILO is therefore ineligible primarily on ILO Eligibility grounds.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call states applicants must be 'legal entities' and Canadian organizations must have a CRA number. The guidance references contribution agreements with standard legal entity requirements. No mention is made of international organizations or UN agencies as eligible applicants. The requirement for a CRA number and standard NGO/civil society framing effectively excludes the ILO as a UN intergovernmental organization.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The mechanism explicitly refers to 'contribution agreements' and non-reimbursable grants/contributions for international development projects. A repayable/innovative finance component is mentioned as an exception, but the primary instrument is a non-reimbursable contribution.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No minimum or maximum grant size is stated anywhere in the main page or guidance documents provided.

Deadline is future.evidence: This is a rolling/unsolicited mechanism with no fixed submission deadline. Applications can be submitted at any time through the Partners@International portal. No specific deadline date is mentioned.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a grant/contribution mechanism for international development projects, not a procurement, RFP, or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No co-financing or cost-sharing requirement is mentioned in the main page or guidance documents.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required. Applicants must register in the Partners@International portal, which is GAC's own system.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium leadership rules or restrictions are mentioned in the available text.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The call is open to international organizations and not restricted to Canadian-registered entities only, though Canadian organizations must have a CRA number. The call funds international development projects globally.

results.proposal_summary: Global Affairs Canada (GAC) operates a rolling unsolicited concept note mechanism for international assistance grants aligned with Canada's Feminist International Assistance Policy. There is no fixed submission deadline; organizations submit concept notes at any time through the Partners@International portal. If a concept note is of interest, GAC invites the applicant to submit a full proposal. Eligible applicants are legal entities (Canadian organizations must have a CRA number); UN agencies and international organizations are not explicitly included. No grant size minimum or maximum is specified, and amounts must be quoted in Canadian dollars. The geographic scope of funded projects is global (developing countries). The two-stage process involves a concept note followed by a full proposal invitation; funding is not guaranteed until a contribution agreement is signed. The ILO, as a UN intergovernmental organization, does not clearly fit the implied eligible applicant categories.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While GAC's Feminist International Assistance Policy can encompass labour rights, gender equality at work, and social protection themes relevant to ILO's mandate, the funding mechanism is primarily designed for NGOs and legal entities rather than UN agencies, and the CfP does not specifically target ILO core themes such as labour standards, employment policy, or social dialogue.

results.exclusion_reason: This page describes Canada's Innovative Finance Programs (International Assistance Innovation Program, Sovereign Loans Program, Conditionally Repayable Contributions) managed by Global Affairs Canada. These are not open calls for proposals/grants but rather program information pages. The funding instruments described include sovereign loans and conditionally repayable contributions (which create financial liabilities/repayment obligations), not non-reimbursable grants. No specific deadline for applications is provided, no grant size information is available, and ILO as an international organization is not listed as an eligible applicant type. The page does not constitute an active Call for Proposals.
results.classification_summary: This is a Canadian government (Global Affairs Canada) program information page describing innovative finance instruments including loans, repayable contributions, and private finance mobilization tools. It is not an active CfP with a defined deadline, grant amount, or eligibility criteria for external applicants. The funding instruments include reimbursable/repayable components. ILO eligibility cannot be confirmed as no eligible applicant categories are defined.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: No eligible applicant categories are defined on this page. The programs appear targeted at partner countries and international development organizations for loans/repayable contributions, not open grant calls where ILO could apply as an implementing partner.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The page describes sovereign loans and conditionally repayable contributions, which create financial liabilities and repayment obligations. While there is mention of an 'International Assistance Innovation Program' that may include non-reimbursable components, no purely non-reimbursable grant window is clearly defined as open for applications.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No specific grant amounts or funding thresholds are stated on this informational page.

Deadline is future.evidence: No submission deadline is provided anywhere on this page. This is a program information/overview page, not an active call for proposals with a defined deadline.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: No RFP, ITB, or procurement/tender language found. The page describes financing programs rather than procurement of goods or services.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The page mentions mobilizing private finance and partnerships but does not explicitly state co-financing as a mandatory requirement for applicants.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific accreditation requirements are mentioned in the available text.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: No consortium or partnership leadership rules are mentioned on this page.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: No explicit geographic restrictions on applicant origin are stated, though the programs are managed by Global Affairs Canada and appear oriented toward international development contexts.

results.proposal_summary: This Global Affairs Canada page provides an overview of two 5-year innovative finance pilot programs: the International Assistance Innovation Program and the Sovereign Loans Program. The programs aim to diversify Canada's international assistance toolkit and support achievement of the UN SDGs by offering flexible financing arrangements including sovereign loans, conditionally repayable contributions, and private finance mobilization tools. The page is informational and does not constitute an active Call for Proposals; no submission deadline, grant size, or eligible applicant categories are specified. The primary funding instruments described include reimbursable and conditionally repayable contributions, making this opportunity ineligible for ILO as there is no clearly defined non-reimbursable grant window with an open application process.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While Canada's international assistance programs broadly support sustainable development goals, this page describes innovative finance mechanisms (loans, repayable contributions) with no specific focus on labour standards, employment, social protection, or other ILO mandate areas, and is not an active grant call.

The stated average project size of approximately CAD 31,000 falls below the USD 50,000 minimum grant threshold. While intergovernmental and multilateral organizations are listed as eligible applicant types, the two structural disqualifiers — aggregator page format and sub-threshold grant size — render the ILO ineligible.

results.proposal_summary: The Canada Fund for Local Initiatives (CFLI), funded by the Government of Canada, supports small, high-impact projects in over 120 ODA-eligible countries with an annual budget of CAD 26.8 million and projects averaging approximately CAD 31,000 each. The programme funds local civil society organizations — including local NGOs, community groups, non-profits, academic institutions, international NGOs, intergovernmental and multilateral organizations, and government agencies — on themes including inclusive governance, gender equality, peace and security, economic growth, environmental action, and human dignity. Eligible applicants include intergovernmental and multilateral organizations, though most funding is directed to local civil society entities. Applications are submitted to the relevant Canadian Embassy or High Commission in each recipient country, with country-specific deadlines ranging from May to June 2026 for currently open calls. The page is an aggregator index listing calls across more than 120 countries, and the average grant size of ~CAD 31,000 falls below the USD 50,000 minimum threshold.
results.ilo_match_evidence: Some CFLI thematic priorities (fair jobs, women's economic rights, entrepreneurship, social protection) partially align with the ILO mandate on decent work and employment, but the programme's primary focus is on local civil society, governance, and humanitarian issues rather than core ILO areas such as labour standards, social dialogue, or occupational safety.

results.exclusion_reason: Deadline passed: the concept note submission deadline was April 16, 2025, which is before today's date of May 11, 2026. Additionally, the call is explicitly closed.

Second, even setting aside the deadline, the maximum individual grant of CAD $10,000,000 (~USD 7.3M) would clear the threshold, but the mandatory 5% cost-sharing requirement is noted as a flagging concern. The decisive exclusion is the past deadline making the call closed.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The page's organization eligibility section is truncated and does not fully enumerate eligible entity types, so it cannot be determined whether UN agencies/intergovernmental organizations are explicitly included or excluded.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline was 12 pm EST April 16, 2025, which is before today's date of May 11, 2026. The page explicitly states 'This funding opportunity process is now closed.'

Is open call with active application win.evidence: The page explicitly states 'This funding opportunity process is now closed' and the submission deadline of April 16, 2025 has passed.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a call for concept notes for project grants, not a procurement or consultancy tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: The attached document states 'Your organization will provide at least 5% of the total eligible direct project costs over the life of the project in cash and/or in-kind,' indicating mandatory cost-sharing.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with a body such as GEF or Green Climate Fund is mentioned as a requirement.

results.proposal_summary: Global Affairs Canada (GAC) issued a call for concept notes for projects supporting health, education, and women's and girls' rights and empowerment in Afghanistan. The total funding envelope was up to CAD $35,000,000 over 4 years, with individual projects expected to range from CAD $2,000,000 to CAD $10,000,000. results.classification_summary: This page is an informational update from Sida regarding its CSO Strategy 2025–2029, clarifying decisions on eligibility of party-affiliated organisations, definition of own funds, and contract lengths. It is not an active call for proposals. The first call was conducted in 2024 and a new call is planned for 2027. The ILO, as an international/intergovernmental organisation and UN agency, does not fall within the eligible applicant category. No active submission deadline exists.

The text explicitly discusses eligibility criteria for CSOs and party-affiliated organisations, with no mention of international organisations or UN agencies as eligible applicants. ILO does not fit the eligible applicant category.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: Sida's CSO strategy provides non-reimbursable grant support to civil society organisations, as indicated by references to 'support', 'agreements', and 'funding' without mention of loans or repayment obligations.

results.proposal_summary: This page from Sida (Sweden's development cooperation agency) provides an informational update — not an active call for proposals — regarding the CSO Strategy 2025–2029. The update clarifies three key decisions: party-affiliated organisations (PAOs) are excluded from eligibility under this strategy; own funds (15% co-financing requirement) must consist solely of privately raised resources; and three-year agreements with extension options are permitted despite the strategy's flexibility requirements. The first call for proposals was conducted in 2024, and applicants were informed of indicative amounts and contract lengths in June 2025. A new call for major support is planned for 2027 with agreements expected from 2028. Eligible applicants are civil society organisations only; international organisations such as the ILO are not eligible. No active submission deadline exists.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While Sida's CSO strategy broadly supports civil society and development cooperation that may touch on ILO-related themes such as rights and equality, this call is exclusively for civil society organisations and does not align with ILO's mandate or eligibility as an applicant.

Second, Phase 1 of the 2026 call is explicitly marked as 'Closed – for information only', confirming the mandatory first-stage deadline has already passed.

results.proposal_summary: The Danida Fellowship Centre, on behalf of the Danish Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Danida), issued a two-phase call for development research grant applications for 2026. Eligible applicants are exclusively universities and research institutions in Denmark. Grants of up to 10 million DKK support collaborative research projects of up to five years' duration, addressing thematic priorities under Denmark's development cooperation strategies ('A Changing World – Partnerships in Development' and 'Africa's Century'), with a focus on Danida priority partner countries in the developing world. Phase 1 (prequalification) is now closed; those prequalified are invited to submit full proposals in Phase 2, with funded projects expected to start in April 2027. The ILO is ineligible as a direct applicant due to the strict restriction to Danish universities and research institutions.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call funds development research broadly aligned with sustainable development, which may tangentially touch ILO-relevant themes (employment, social protection, just transition), but the primary focus is academic research in Danish institutions with no specific mandate linked to ILO's core areas of labour standards, social dialogue, or decent work.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO Eligibility: The call targets research institutions/universities in Denmark and partner countries; ILO as an international organization does not fit the eligible applicant categories. Deadline: The deadline for Phase 2 is not explicitly stated in the page content, but the call is described as Phase 2 with documents dated May 2025 — however, the deadline cannot be confirmed as future without explicit date. Additionally, the eligible applicant types exclude ILO.
results.classification_summary: This call by Danida Fellowship Centre funds collaborative development research projects between Danish and partner-country research institutions. The eligible applicants are research institutions and universities, not international organizations such as the ILO. Because the ILO does not fit within the exhaustive list of eligible entity types defined by this call, it is ineligible. No explicit submission deadline date is found in the provided content, making the deadline status unknown, but the ILO eligibility exclusion alone is sufficient to render this call ineligible.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call targets Danish and partner-country research institutions/universities. The assessment criteria and guidelines reference 'PI' (Principal Investigator), research teams, and North-South research partnerships between academic institutions. International organizations such as the ILO are not listed as eligible applicants, and the exhaustive framing excludes them.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call is titled 'Research Grants' and the Danida Fellowship Centre provides non-reimbursable research grants funded by the Danish Ministry of Foreign Affairs (Danida).

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No specific grant size or funding range is stated in the provided page content or attached documents.

Deadline is future.evidence: No explicit submission deadline date is stated in the provided page content. Documents are dated May 2025 but no closing date for Phase 2 applications is visible in the extracted text.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: This is a research grant call, not a procurement or consultancy tender. The documents explicitly distinguish research from consultancy and development projects.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: No co-financing or cost-sharing requirement is mentioned in the available content.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body is required according to the available content.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The call references North-South research partnerships between universities/research institutions, but no explicit rule requiring consortium leadership by a non-UN entity is stated.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: The call requires a Danish research institution as the lead partner (North partner), with collaboration from institutions in Danida partner countries. This restricts applicants to Denmark-based institutions in the lead role.

The call supports genuine collaborative research projects between Danish universities/research institutions (North) and partner-country research institutions (South) in Danida's priority developing countries. Projects must address the call's thematic focus, generate new knowledge, contribute to the SDGs, and include substantial research capacity strengthening in the South. Eligible applicants are Danish-led research consortia with Southern partners; international organizations such as the ILO are not eligible. The grant amount is not specified in available content. No explicit submission deadline is visible in the provided text. The call requires equitable North-South partnerships, gender sensitivity, and relevance to Danish development cooperation priorities.

results.proposal_summary: IFAD is seeking an implementing partner for a grant titled 'Evidence for Scale-Up of AgTech and FinTech Solutions through IFAD's PoLG' (Reference No. 2000005624), published on 21 April 2026. The grant aims to generate robust evidence and lessons learned to support the expansion of financial and non-financial services for rural smallholders delivered by AgTech and FinTech companies under IFAD's Innovatech programme, including ex-post evaluation in Latin America and the Caribbean (Innovatech 1.0 and 2.0) and ex-ante assessment in Near East, North Africa, Europe, and Central Asia (Jordan, Uzbekistan, Turkey). The implementing partner will also develop an investor-oriented impact reporting methodology. Eligible applicants are non-profit organizations, NGOs, and commercial entities; UN agencies/international organizations are not listed as eligible. All proposals must include at least 20% co-financing (25% for private sector), and grant budget amounts are not specified. The deadline for submission is 13 May 2026 (Rome time, UTC+2). A Q&A session is scheduled for 4 May 2026 (per LoI) or 6 May 2026 (per ToR). Proposals must be submitted in English to lacgrants@ifad.org.

results.proposal_summary: The United Nations Road Safety Fund (UNRSF) issued a 2024 Call for Proposals targeting Participating UN Organisations (PUNOs) to submit project proposals addressing road safety priorities. The call focused on two funding priorities: (1) regional programmes implementing road safety laws and standards aligned with UN legal instruments in at least three countries, including motorcycle safety; and (2) domestic road safety financing strategies to help governments develop sustainable national financing schemes. The call was open globally to PUNOs, with ILO, WHO, UNDP, UNICEF, and numerous other UN entities participating. The call is now closed, with engagement strategies from all participating organisations already published on the UNRSF website as of 2024. No active application window or future deadline is currently available.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While ILO participated in this UNRSF 2024 Call for Proposals with its own engagement strategy on road safety, road safety is only peripherally related to the ILO's core mandate of labour standards, employment, and decent work, and the call is no longer active.

results.proposal_summary: The Resilient and Inclusive Cities Hub (RICH), hosted by the Global Disability Fund (GDF) with seed funding from Germany's BMZ, aims to create a global network of disability-inclusive cities by piloting inclusive urban resilience solutions, fostering multi-stakeholder collaboration, and generating best practices for accessible, equitable, and sustainable urban development. The initiative targets cities worldwide, with planned pilot projects in five cities by 2026. No active call for proposals is currently open; interested organisations are invited to subscribe to a mailing list to be notified of future funding calls. No grant amounts, eligibility criteria, or submission deadlines have been published.
results.ilo_match_evidence: RICH addresses urban inclusion and resilience for persons with disabilities, which intersects partially with ILO themes of working conditions, equality, and social protection, but does not focus on core ILO mandate areas such as labour standards, employment policy, or social dialogue.

Additionally, no submission deadline is present, making the deadline criterion unknown, and the opportunity appears to be an investment facility seeking investors rather than a grant call for project implementers.
results.classification_summary: The Cabo Verde Blu-X Facility 2.0 is an investment facility (not a grant call) managed by the Joint SDG Fund, seeking $50M in blended finance through blue bonds and impact investments to develop Cabo Verde's blue economy and sustainable tourism. The fund offers a 4%+ coupon yield, meaning returns must be paid to investors — this is a debt/financial instrument, not a non-reimbursable grant. There is no application deadline, no eligible applicant categories defined for project grants, and the call is directed at institutional investors, DFIs, and foundations rather than implementing organisations. ILO, as a UN agency seeking grant funding, does not fit this opportunity.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The opportunity is directed at institutional ESG investors, DFIs, impact investors, sovereign wealth funds, and diaspora networks as capital providers. No section defines eligible applicants for grant-based project implementation; ILO as an international organisation/UN agency does not fit any listed investor category.

results.proposal_summary: The DigiKen Facility is a Joint SDG Fund blended finance investment vehicle targeting $55M to scale Kenya's digital economy, with the goal of catalyzing $275M+ in additional public and private investment. Focused on Kenya (Africa's Silicon Savannah), it aims to support 5,000+ MSMEs, create 50,000+ technology jobs, and empower 500,000+ women, youth, and marginalized communities. The facility offers financial returns of 4-6% on guarantees and 15-22% IRR on digital platform ventures over a 4-5 year period. Eligible participants are investors such as DFIs, impact investors, and private sector entities — not grant recipients. There is no submission deadline stated and no grant component; the ILO is not eligible as this is a pure investment/blended finance solicitation.

results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Office to Monitor and Combat Trafficking in Persons (TIP Office) issued a Notice of Intent on December 3, 2024, indicating its plan to competitively award cooperative agreements under the Program to End Modern Slavery (PEMS) FY 2025. The program, funded at $25 million annually by Congress since 2016, aims to measurably reduce the prevalence of human trafficking through innovative, evidence-based interventions including forced labor and sex trafficking. Awards will support implementation and rigorous evaluation of promising anti-trafficking interventions. Geographic focus, individual award sizes, and a formal submission deadline have not yet been established. The TIP Office is not currently accepting applications, and a full solicitation has not been released. Eligible applicants are broadly described as 'organizations' with local expertise encouraged and partnerships with international organizations welcomed.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The program targets forced labor and human trafficking, which directly intersects with ILO's mandate on fundamental principles and rights at work, including elimination of forced labour, though the broader anti-trafficking focus goes beyond core ILO labour standards work.

Furthermore, the CfP's subject matter—internet censorship circumvention, digital security, and online repression—falls entirely outside the ILO's mandate on labour standards, employment, and social protection. The program is ineligible for ILO on both applicant eligibility and thematic grounds.

results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Bureau of Democracy, Human Rights, and Labor (DRL) issued an Annual Program Statement in October 2024 inviting Statements of Interest (SOIs) from organizations implementing Internet Freedom programs. The program supports four themes: (1) directly circumventing or defeating internet censorship through technology; (2) preventing and remedying online attacks through digital security; (3) advocating against repressive internet laws; and (4) researching and documenting threats to internet freedom. Priority regions include Africa, East Asia and the Pacific, North Africa and the Middle East, South and Central Asia, and the Western Hemisphere. No specific grant size is stated. Programs are expected to begin no earlier than August or September 2025. Eligible applicants are civil society organizations, NGOs, and implementing partners; the ILO as an international organization does not fit the eligible applicant category. The SOI submission deadline is not explicitly stated in the available text.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This CfP focuses exclusively on internet freedom, anti-censorship technology, and digital security, which fall outside the ILO's core mandate on labour standards, employment, social protection, and decent work.

results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Bureau of Democracy, Human Rights and Labor (DRL) has issued standing Proposal Submission Instructions (updated January 2020) governing applications to its grant programs. Eligible applicants include U.S.- and foreign-based NGOs, public international organizations (including the ILO), academic institutions, and for-profit entities, with a preference for non-profits. The instructions apply globally across individual Notices of Funding Opportunities (NOFOs) issued by DRL, each of which will specify countries, thematic focus, funding amounts, and deadlines. Funding is provided as non-reimbursable assistance awards (grants), and co-financing is explicitly not required. Applications are submitted through the SAMS Domestic portal and must include detailed narratives, budgets, logic models, monitoring and evaluation plans, risk analyses, and gender analyses. No overarching grant size or implementation period is specified in these instructions.
results.ilo_match_evidence: DRL funds democracy, human rights, and labor rights programs globally, and the labor rights dimension directly overlaps with ILO's mandate on fundamental principles and rights at work, though the focus is broader than ILO's core mandate.

results.proposal_summary: The U.S. Department of State's Office to Monitor and Combat Trafficking in Persons (TIP Office) runs an annual competitive grant program funding programs to combat human trafficking globally (outside the United States). Eligible applicants include U.S.-based and foreign NGOs, non-profits, for-profit organizations (no profit generation permitted), higher education institutions, public international organizations (including UN agencies such as the ILO), and U.S. government agencies; foreign governments are excluded. Awards have typically reached up to USD 1,000,000 for two to three years, with larger and longer-term proposals welcomed. The two-stage process starts with a Statement of Interest in response to a NOFO posted on SAMS Domestic and Grants.gov; no specific deadline is stated on the general process page. Projects must address prosecution, protection, or prevention of sex and/or labor trafficking, incorporate survivor input, and align with U.S. government priorities. Contact: JTIPGrants@state.gov.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The grant directly targets labor trafficking and forced labour—core ILO mandate areas under fundamental principles and rights at work—and the ILO as a PIO is explicitly eligible to apply.
