results.proposal_summary: This URL points to the AFD (Agence Française de Développement) calls-for-projects listing page, which aggregates multiple calls across various themes including employment, decent work, gender equality, education, health, and social protection. As of 2026-04-22, all listed calls on this page are marked as 'Closed call' with deadlines that have passed (the most recent being May 6, 2025). No specific active or upcoming call is described with sufficient detail to perform a full eligibility assessment. The page is therefore not actionable as a current funding opportunity.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The AFD listing page includes themes such as Employment, Decent Work, and Social Protection that align with ILO mandate areas, but no specific active call targeting these themes is currently open.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO (as an International Organization/UN agency) is not eligible: the eligible applicant types are strictly limited to CSOs/NGOs (French associations, syndicates, foundations, local civil society organizations, and non-governmental organizations). Additionally, no single active call with a clear future deadline is identifiable — all specific FISONG and APCC calls referenced (2018, 2021, 2022) have deadlines well in the past relative to today (2026-04-22). The CSO Initiatives Facility opens mid-March to mid-May each year, but no 2026 call with a confirmed future deadline is documented in the source.
results.classification_summary: This AFD page describes multiple financing mechanisms for CSOs/NGOs (CSO Initiatives Facility, FISONG, APCC, FSOF, small-scale programs). Eligible applicants are strictly civil society organizations — French associations, foundations, syndicates, and local NGOs — explicitly excluding international organizations such as the ILO. All specific calls referenced have past deadlines. No active 2026 call with a future deadline is documented. The ILO fails both the ILO Eligibility and Deadline is future criteria.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The page explicitly defines eligible applicants as French associations (Law 1901), French syndicates, French foundations, local CSOs in ODA-eligible countries, and NGOs. The ILO as a UN international organization does not fit any of these categories. FISONG Q&A also confirms 'A Non-Governmental Organization means any public interest organization which is not linked to a State or to an international institution,' explicitly excluding international institutions like the ILO.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The page describes co-financing via grants (subventions), with AFD providing non-reimbursable contributions covering up to 80–90% of project costs for CSOs.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: CSO Initiatives Facility requires total project amount ≥ €500,000; FISONG 2021 mentions projects of €700k–€1M per project, both well above the USD 50,000 threshold.

Deadline is future.evidence: All specific calls referenced have past deadlines: FISONG 2018 (deadline early 2018), FISONG 2020, FISONG 2021, FISONG 2022, APCC 2022, FSOF 2022. The CSO Initiatives Facility opens annually mid-March to mid-May but no confirmed 2026 call with a future deadline is documented in the source text. The FISONG was suspended in 2023. No active call with a deadline after 2026-04-22 is identified.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The page describes grant mechanisms and calls for project proposals for CSOs, not procurement of goods/services or consultancy tenders.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: Co-financing is mandatory: FISONG requires NGOs to contribute at least 10% of the total project budget (and this cannot be in-kind). The CSO Initiatives Facility also specifies maximum AFD co-financing rates (80% for French CSOs, 90% for local CSOs), implying mandatory co-financing of at least 10–20%.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with a third-party body (e.g., GEF, Green Climate Fund) is required. Eligibility is based on organizational type, age, and track record.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: The lead applicant must be an NGO (non-governmental organization). FISONG Q&A confirms 'The lead applicant has to be a non-governmental organization (NGO),' which explicitly excludes UN/international organizations from leading consortia.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: While French CSOs are the primary target for the CSO Initiatives Facility, FISONG is open to international NGOs of any nationality, and the APCC is open to international CSOs. Geographic restriction does not apply uniformly across all mechanisms.

results.proposal_summary: The Agence Française de Développement (AFD), on behalf of the French Government, operates several grant financing mechanisms for civil society organizations (CSOs) and NGOs working in international solidarity and development. The main instruments include the CSO Initiatives Facility (for projects ≥ €500,000 in all OECD DAC ODA-eligible countries, co-financing up to 80–90% of project costs), the now-suspended FISONG innovation facility (open to international NGOs of any nationality, funding €700k–€1M per project), the Crisis and Post-Crisis Calls for Projects (APCC, funded via Minka Fund and EU trust funds), and the Support Fund for Feminist Organizations (FSOF). Eligible applicants are strictly CSOs/NGOs — French associations, syndicates, foundations, and local civil society organizations — explicitly excluding international organizations such as the ILO. All specific calls documented on this page have closed deadlines (2018–2022), and the FISONG has been suspended since 2023. The CSO Initiatives Facility reopens annually mid-March to mid-May but no confirmed 2026 active call is documented. Mandatory co-financing of at least 10% is required for most instruments.
results.ilo_match_evidence: While some past FISONG themes (social protection, financial inclusion of migrants, informal economy) align with ILO's mandate, the ILO is explicitly ineligible as an international institution under all AFD CSO financing mechanisms, making this opportunity irrelevant for ILO regardless of thematic alignment.

results.proposal_summary: Switzerland's State Secretariat for Economic Affairs (SECO) launched its second call for proposals on 9 January 2026, inviting Swiss companies operating in Ukraine to submit reconstruction project proposals addressing Ukraine's key needs, with a focus on public infrastructure. Up to CHF 150 million is available under the call, with individual project amounts ranging from CHF 3 million to CHF 30 million, funded from the CHF 500 million envelope for Swiss private-sector projects under Switzerland's Ukraine Country Programme 2025–2028. Eligible applicants are exclusively Swiss companies; proposals were to be submitted by 8 March 2026. A two-stage process was used: initial project ideas were screened against predefined criteria, after which shortlisted companies were invited to submit fully developed proposals followed by interviews.
results.ilo_match_evidence: The call targets private-sector infrastructure reconstruction in Ukraine and has no substantive focus on labour standards, employment policy, social protection, or other core ILO mandate areas.

results.exclusion_reason: ILO is not an eligible applicant type. The call explicitly targets private sector entities, CSOs (Lot 1), and Caribbean-based scientists/research institutions (Lot 2). No mention of UN agencies, international organisations, or IGOs as eligible applicants.
results.classification_summary: This call by Expertise France targets private sector entities and CSOs for sargassum value chain initiatives (Lot 1), and Caribbean-based scientists and research institutions for sargassum knowledge production (Lot 2). The eligible applicant categories are exhaustive and do not include UN agencies, intergovernmental organisations, or multilateral entities, rendering the ILO ineligible. Co-financing is mandatory (50% of total eligible costs for most applicants). The deadline of 19 May 2026 is in the future and the call is currently open.

ILO Eligibility.evidence: The call explicitly names eligible applicants as 'private sector entities and Civil Society Organizations (CSOs)' (Lot 1) and 'Caribbean-based scientists and research institutions' (Lot 2). UN agencies, intergovernmental organisations, or IGOs are not listed as eligible applicant types.

Funding instrument is grant.evidence: The call is titled 'Grant awards' and references grant contracts with non-reimbursable contributions from Expertise France.

Grant size above threshold.evidence: No explicit minimum or maximum grant amount is stated in the visible page content. The audit threshold of EUR 750,000 is mentioned as a reference for documentation, not as a grant ceiling.

Deadline is future.evidence: The deadline is 19 May 2026 at 19:00 (Paris time), which is after today's date of 2026-05-04.

Is open call with active application win.evidence: A single specific call is described with a dedicated portal, application mechanism ('Répondre'), and a future deadline of 19 May 2026. The call opened on 20 March 2026.

Is procurement or tender.evidence: The call is explicitly titled 'Grant awards' and described as an 'Appel à projets ouvert' (open call for projects), not a procurement or tender.

Requires cofinancing.evidence: Co-financing is mandatory: grants must not exceed 50% of total eligible costs (or 90% for CSO lead applicants in Lot 1). The balance must be financed from sources other than EU or Expertise France budgets. Expertise France does not accept co-financing in kind.

Requires specific accreditation.evidence: No specific pre-existing accreditation with another body (e.g., GEF, Green Climate Fund) is required.

Has consortium leadership rules.evidence: Consortia are not permitted; only a single lead applicant with partners is allowed. No specific rule requiring non-UN consortium leadership is mentioned.

Applicant origin is restricted.evidence: While projects must be implemented in specified Caribbean countries, the Q&A confirms that lead applicants and partners may be registered outside Caribbean countries or territories.

results.proposal_summary: Expertise France, under the SARSEA programme, is launching a call for projects targeting two lots: Lot 1 supports private sector entities and CSOs in the development of innovative and sustainable sargassum value chain initiatives in the Commonwealth of Dominica, Grenada, Saint Vincent and the Grenadines, and Saint Lucia; Lot 2 supports Caribbean-based scientists and research institutions in producing, disseminating, and applying knowledge related to sargassum management, valorization, and health-related impacts across a wider set of countries including Guadeloupe, Martinique, Mexico, Belize, and the Dominican Republic. Grants cover a maximum of 50% of total eligible costs (or 90% for CSO lead applicants; no co-funding required for PhD/postdoctoral projects). The deadline for submission is 19 May 2026 at 19:00 Paris time. The ILO is not an eligible applicant as the call is restricted to private sector entities, CSOs, and research institutions/scientists.
results.ilo_match_evidence: This call focuses on sargassum environmental management and value chain development, which has minimal overlap with the ILO's core mandate on labour standards, employment, social protection, and decent work.
